aliases: 韩式烤牛短排 (Hánshì kǎo niú duǎn pái)

chinese: 韩式烤牛短排 (Hánshì kǎo niú duǎn pái)

food_subgroup: Korean marinated beef short ribs (cross-cut, flanken-style), grilled over charcoal; marinade (양념) of soy sauce, Asian pear, garlic, ginger, sesame oil, and sugar

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for galbi as a distinct Korean marinated beef short rib dish. Closest parent would be a generic grilled beef dish node.
ontology.foodex2_description: Marinated grilled beef short ribs, Korean-style (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code for galbi)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0RBB used as closest parent for marinated grilled beef; no exact galbi code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for galbi as a specific Korean dish.

serving.description: 1 serving: 4 short rib pieces (~200g with bone, ~130g actual cooked meat)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Korean BBQ restaurant serving of galbi is 4 cross-cut short rib pieces weighing approximately 200g with bone, yielding approximately 130g of cooked meat after discounting bone weight.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, short ribs, cross-cut, choice, separable lean and fat, cooked, braised — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 174055
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FDC SR Legacy FDC 174055 (beef short ribs, cross-cut, choice, cooked) used as the primary nutrient anchor for the beef component. Values adjusted upward for marinade contribution (soy sauce sodium, pear/sugar carbohydrate ~7.7g/100g, sesame oil fat). Korea Food Research Institute (KFRI) data used as corroborating reference for the marinated and grilled preparation. Per-100g values represent the cooked, marinated, grilled product including marinade absorption.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimation for Korean marinated grilled beef short ribs based on analogous high-protein, moderate-carbohydrate grilled meat with soy-sugar marinade; reference: University of Sydney GI Database — beef and soy-marinated meat analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for galbi in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~35 is estimated based on the food's macronutrient profile: predominantly protein (21.5g/100g) and fat (20.8g/100g) with only ~7.7g/100g carbohydrate from the soy-pear-sugar marinade. The low carbohydrate content and high fat/protein ratio strongly attenuate glycaemic response. Analogous soy-marinated grilled beef dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database support a GI in the low range (≤55). GL per 130g serving = (35 × 10g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 3.5, rounded to 3.2 per input source.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium from soy sauce marinade: ~600mg per 130g serving (462mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Galbi contributes approximately 600mg sodium per standard serving primarily from the soy sauce marinade; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid additional high-sodium banchan (side dishes) or dipping sauces in the same meal, as a full Korean BBQ sitting can easily exceed 2,000–3,000mg sodium total.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycaemic load (~3.2 per serving); carbohydrate from marinade only (~10g per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Galbi has a very low glycaemic load of approximately 3.2 per standard 130g serving, making it a suitable protein-dominant choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the ~10g marinade-derived carbohydrate is easily accounted for in carbohydrate counting, and the high fat content (~27g per serving) further slows gastric emptying and moderates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat: 8.5g/100g (~11g per serving); trans fat 0.3g/100g; cholesterol 78mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Galbi is a high-saturated-fat dish (~11g SFA per serving from intramuscular and inter-rib fat), which may raise LDL cholesterol with frequent consumption; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption, choose leaner cuts when possible, and balance with vegetables and lower-fat protein sources within the same meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High SFA (pro-inflammatory at chronic high intake) offset by zinc (5.5mg/100g, ~7.2mg/serving) and haem iron (2.0mg/100g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Galbi's high saturated fat content may be pro-inflammatory with chronic high intake in rheumatoid arthritis; however, its high zinc content (~7.2mg per serving) supports immune function relevant to RA, and its haem iron (~2.6mg per serving at ~25% bioavailability) is clinically relevant for RA-associated anaemia.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Galbi (갈비 / 소갈비) is a traditional Korean grilled beef short rib dish, one of the most iconic items in Korean BBQ cuisine. The dish uses cross-cut (flanken-style) beef short ribs, marinated in a classic Korean yangnyeom (양념) sauce of soy sauce, Asian pear (which acts as a natural meat tenderiser via actinidin protease activity), garlic, ginger, sesame oil, and sugar, then grilled over charcoal. A standard restaurant serving is 4 short rib pieces (~200g with bone, yielding ~130g cooked meat). Per 130g serving: approximately 425 kcal, 28g protein, 27g fat (11g saturated fat from intramuscular and inter-rib fat), 10g carbohydrate (from marinade pear sugars and added sugar), and 600mg sodium (from soy sauce). Glycaemic load is very low (~3.2 per serving; GI ~35), making it suitable for blood glucose management when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments. The charcoal-grilling process produces characteristic smoky flavour and surface char (with associated HCA formation at char surfaces). Galbi is a premium Korean BBQ item served at restaurants such as Bornga, Gen Korean BBQ, and Bulgogi Brothers in Singapore. It is dairy-free and rich in zinc (5.5mg/100g), vitamin B12 (1.8mcg/100g), and haem iron (2.0mg/100g). The dish is documented in Joseon dynasty Korean cookbooks and remains a centrepiece of Korean celebratory and social dining.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are anchored to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC 174055 (beef short ribs, cross-cut, choice, cooked), a primary approved source, with marinade adjustments corroborated by Korea Food Research Institute (KFRI) data. The food is well-characterised in the literature. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, with confidence 0.9 per input source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for galbi as a marinated grilled preparation is not separately reported in USDA FDC 174055 at the required granularity for this composite dish; values are not available from USDA FoodData Central for the marinated-and-grilled product as a whole. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the cooked marinated product is not reported in the primary source at the per-100g level for this preparation. trans_fat_g of 0.3g/100g reflects naturally occurring ruminant trans fats (vaccenic acid / conjugated linoleic acid) present in beef, not industrial trans fats; this is consistent with USDA FDC 174055 data for beef short ribs. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero: beef contains no dietary fibre; the marinade ingredients (soy sauce, pear juice, garlic, ginger in small quantities) contribute negligible fibre to the final dish per USDA FDC data. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for galbi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (35 × 10g) / 100 = 3.5; input source reports 3.2 (minor rounding difference retained from input). KFRI is cited as a corroborating reference; primary nutrient anchor for this output is USDA FDC SR Legacy FDC 174055 as an approved source per Project Veritas source policy.
